question.text: What is a Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)?

answer.text: A facility to withdraw a fixed amount at regular intervals from a mutual fund scheme
A plan to withdraw all units at once from a mutual fund
A penalty-free withdrawal scheme for senior citizens
An automatic closure of the mutual fund account

explanation.text: **The Logic:** SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan) allows an investor to withdraw a fixed amount at regular intervals (monthly, quarterly, etc.) from their mutual fund investment. The fund house redeems the required number of units to pay the specified amount. This is very popular among retirees who want regular monthly income from their mutual fund corpus. **The Trap:** SWP is not "interest" or "income" from the fund. It is a partial redemption of units. If the withdrawal amount exceeds the returns earned, the capital will gradually reduce. It is important to set a sustainable withdrawal rate. **Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in V-A and Series XXIV. Very important for retirees. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** SWP is one of the most important products for retired investors. From a tax perspective, each SWP withdrawal is treated as a redemption, so only the capital gains portion (not the full amount) is taxable. This makes SWP from the Growth option more tax-efficient than the IDCW option.
